title: Final Best Picture Power Rankings and How Oscar Voting Really Works
format: Sean Fennessey and Amanda Dobbins clear the last awards-season precursors (a Netflix-hosted SAG telecast, the PGA, the Independent Spirits, BAFTA, the WGA nominees, the Cesars), then lock a final Best Picture power ranking with Oppenheimer uncatchable and The Zone of Interest the only plausible spoiler. Then former Academy awards administrator Tom Oyer walks through how the voting actually works: preferential Best Picture ballots, proportional ranked-choice nominations by branch, and why he won't use the word 'snubbed.'

segments.heading: The last precursors
segments.note: SAG, PGA, the Spirits, BAFTA, WGA, the Cesars

films.title: Spider-Man: Across the Spider-Verse
films.note: Won Best Animated at the SAG-adjacent precursor slate, leaving a two-horse animated race. Sean is conflicted: the last Spider-Verse film already won, and the Academy tends to reward the finale, not the middle chapter, of a trilogy.

films.title: The Boy and the Heron
films.note: Took the BAFTA for animation, the counterweight to Spider-Verse. Sean floats a possible last Miyazaki movie as the sentimental case, even if it plays as incoherent to voters half-watching on the screener portal.

films.title: American Symphony
films.note: Not nominated, the documentary branch's most-discussed omission. Sean flags the doc branch as the category he can never read and wants to ask Tom Oyer about.

films.title: 20 Days in Mariupol
films.note: Won the BAFTA for documentary and, in Sean's read, the clear frontrunner. He watched it during 'wellness week' and calls it astonishing and at times unwatchable, with a sequence in the first 30 minutes that is hard to cope with. Streaming on PBS.

films.title: Past Lives
films.note: Won Best Feature and Best Director at the Independent Spirit Awards. Cited later as the film Scorsese and Nolan keep singling out, giving it a concentrated base of 'film people' support.

films.title: A Thousand and One
films.note: A.V. Rockwell's Best First Feature winner at the Spirits, another prize Sean wishes the Oscars handed out.

films.title: Air
films.note: A WGA Original nominee that isn't an Oscar screenplay nominee at all, part of a scrambled Guild slate thrown off by the strike-delayed timeline.

films.title: Nyad
films.note: An odd WGA Adapted nominee alongside Oppenheimer and Killers of the Flower Moon; the hosts read the whole Guild lineup as chaotic this year.

films.title: Are You There God? It's Me, Margaret.
films.note: Another WGA Adapted nominee that never entered the Oscar conversation.

films.title: Oppenheimer
films.note: No. 1, and 'untackable.' Golden Globe, BAFTA, Critics' Choice, SAG cast, DGA, and PGA: just the 11th film to sweep SAG, DGA, and PGA together. The kind of front-runner (Slumdog, Return of the King, Gladiator) that makes an upset extremely unlikely. Sean's over/under of 8.5 Oscars from 13 nominations lands on the under.

films.title: The Zone of Interest
films.note: No. 2 on the strength of international support and the moment: Gaza referenced in its BAFTA speeches, Spielberg calling it the best Holocaust film since his own. The hosts agree a win would be stunning but say it's Oppenheimer's likeliest challenger. Amanda floats, then rejects on ethical grounds, betting on it.

films.title: Poor Things
films.note: No. 3. A specialty-box-office success that 'consecrated' Emma Stone even if she loses Best Actress, with strength in production and costume design. The hosts don't think it wins the top prize.

films.title: Anatomy of a Fall
films.note: No. 4, up from ninth in January when the hosts weren't sure it would get in. Cleaned up at the Cesars and has the juice in Original Screenplay, where a non-English winner (Parasite, Talk to Her) is rare but not unheard of.

films.title: The Holdovers
films.note: No. 5. 'How the mighty have fallen' from a January No. 2. Da'Vine Joy Randolph is the one lock, sweeping Supporting Actress everywhere; Giamatti appears to have lost Best Actor to Cillian Murphy.

films.title: Past Lives
films.note: No. 6. Sean argues the affection among voters and 'film people' runs deeper than for Barbie, which is why it edges ahead here despite Barbie's bigger footprint.

films.title: Barbie
films.note: No. 7, with the caveat that ranked-choice makes it either a 10 or a 2 depending on how ballots break: the exact question they save for Tom Oyer. America Ferrera's nomination and Werner Herzog's stray endorsement both come up.

films.title: Killers of the Flower Moon
films.note: No. 8. People think it's too long ('that's on them,' Sean says), and Lily Gladstone's live Best Actress race is the film's one real shot at hardware.

films.title: American Fiction
films.note: No. 9. Cord Jefferson has a real chance in Adapted Screenplay (he won at the Spirits and the BAFTAs), but the film has little juice elsewhere.

films.title: Maestro
films.note: No. 10. 'Really not the award season you wanted if you're on the Maestro team.' The hosts put it last and mean it.

segments.heading: Tom Oyer on how Oscar voting really works

films.title: The Dark Knight
films.note: The standing example for why Best Picture expanded: the film whose omission is credited with pushing the category toward a wider, eventually locked-at-10 lineup.

films.title: Summer of Soul
films.note: Oyer's favorite illustration of ranked-choice working: a documentary lineup (an archival concert film, a verite piece, an animated doc) that could only have come from different pools of voters each backing their passion pick.

films.title: Ascension
films.note: One of the five Documentary Feature nominees Oyer cites as proof the ballot surfaces wildly different styles of filmmaking.

films.title: Attica
films.note: Another of that Documentary Feature five, in Oyer's example of a lineup no single bloc could have produced.

films.title: Flee
films.note: The animated documentary in Oyer's five-nominee example (evidence, he says, that proportional ranked-choice lets divergent tastes each get represented).

films.title: Writing with Fire
films.note: Rounds out Oyer's Documentary Feature lineup, the verite entry among five very different approaches.

films.title: In the Heat of the Night
films.note: Part of Oyer's run through Oscars that match their moment: a 1967 winner about race, followed by pure escapism the next year.

films.title: Oliver!
films.note: The escapist Best Picture winner in 1968, 'such a tumultuous year,' in Oyer's read of how the awards track the social weather.

films.title: Midnight Cowboy
films.note: The X-rated 1969 winner that closes Oyer's late-'60s example of the Academy swinging with the times.
